  "opinion": "As a man in his late eighties, I've seen some things in my time. Social media is one of them, but it's not like the old days when you had to rely solely on word-of-mouth or letters from far away places. Back when I was running fitness courses in Lecce and beyond, we did it properly – face-to-face, no fake content involved! response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion:
As a man in his late eighties, I've seen some things in my time. Social media is one of them, but it's not like the old days when you had to rely solely on word-of-mouth or letters from far away places. Back when I was running fitness courses in Lecce and beyond, we did it properly – face-to-face, no fake content involved! But now, with all this digital noise, misinformation spreads faster than a viral workout routine gone wrong. It's a bit overwhelming for someone like me who prefers traditional ways but still keeps an eye on things because of my job.

My creativity helps me see through the lies sometimes; if something doesn't feel right or genuine to me – and I've got plenty of experience in fitness to know what’s authentic health advice versus snake oil – I tend to ignore it. My moderate level of openness means I don’t jump on every trend, but since my children are grown (though one still follows football), we do use social media occasionally for updates from friends or local events down here by the sea.

I think dealing with fake content requires common sense and a bit of skepticism. Look at sources carefully – is that page professional? Do they have evidence backing their claims? My vocational diploma in fitness training taught me more about discernment than years of formal education ever could.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: First, consider the source for any health-related post on social media. If it's from an official or recognized expert, like a government site or a certified trainer, then likely genuine. Second, cross-reference with reliable sources whenever possible. Third, use common sense – if something sounds too good to be true about losing weight quickly without exercise, steer clear.
